title: $0 Authorization Trial Probe with Immediate High-Value Follow-On Attempt
narrative: Fraudster validates stolen card credentials through legitimate $0.00 trial authorization at streaming or SaaS merchant (ProductCD=W or S), then immediately attempts high-value purchase at different merchant within 15-45 minutes. The trial authorization creates card1_txn_count baseline without actual charge, testing for active card status and available credit. Successful $0 auth triggers rapid pivot to electronics retailer or digital goods merchant with $300-$900 transaction. Pattern exploits merchant assumption that $0 auth failure means card is dead, but fraudster interprets successful authorization as green light. Temporal clustering is key: both transactions occur same session, often same txn_hour, with dist1 distance between billing/shipping addresses jumping from 0km to 500+ km on the second attempt. P_emaildomain switches from legitimate provider (gmail.com) on trial to disposable (tempmail, guerrillamail) on high-value purchase.
indicators: Initial transaction: TransactionAmt = $0.00 with ProductCD = W or S at subscription merchant
Second transaction within 15-45 minutes: TransactionAmt between $300-$900, ProductCD = C or H
card1_txn_count_24h increases from 1 to 2 with no intermediate transactions
dist1 = 0 on first transaction, dist1 > 500 on second transaction
P_emaildomain changes from major provider (gmail/yahoo/outlook) to disposable domain between transactions
Both transactions occur within same txn_hour window (0-23)
card1_amt_zscore spikes from baseline on second transaction (z-score > 3.0)
summary: A $0 trial authorization at a streaming or SaaS service is immediately followed within 15-45 minutes by a $300-$900 purchase at a different merchant, with the billing/shipping distance jumping from 0km to 500+km and the email switching from a legitimate provider to a disposable domain.
reasoning: The fraudster uses the free trial to verify the stolen card is active without triggering fraud alerts, then races to make a high-value purchase before the victim notices, switching to a disposable email and distant shipping address to collect the goods anonymously.
indicator_explanations: Card is tested with zero-dollar authorization at a subscription service
Large purchase follows immediately after the probe validates the card
Card shows exactly two transactions with no activity in between
Purchase location jumps hundreds of miles from the initial test transaction
Email switches from legitimate provider to throwaway domain between attempts
Both test and attack happen within the same hour block
Purchase amount is dramatically larger than cardholder's normal spending pattern
